name: Some special elements of G
statement_text:
If ╬▒ Ōłł G1 such that ╬▒(Žä ) = 1, and m is an integer with
m Ōłł {0, 1, ŌłÆ1}, then ╬▒(m) = ╬▒m implies that ╬▒ = E.
proof_text:
Proof. For any tree t = Žä , we have ╬▒(m) (t) = r(t)m ╬▒(t) + Q1 and ╬▒m (t) =
m╬▒(t) + Q2 , where Q1 and Q2 are expressions involving ╬▒(u) for r(u) < r(t).
Suppose that ╬▒(u) has been proved equal to E(u) for all such trees. Then

                          ╬▒(m) (t) = r(t)m ╬▒(t) + Q1 ,
                           ╬▒m (t) = m╬▒(t) + Q2 ,
                          E (m) (t) = r(t)m E(t) + Q1 ,
                           E m (t) = mE(t) + Q2 ,

so that ╬▒(m) (t) = ╬▒m (t) implies that

                        (r(t)m ŌłÆ m)(╬▒(t) ŌłÆ E(t)) = 0,

implying that ╬▒(t) = E(t), because r(t)m = m whenever r(t) > 1 and
m Ōłł {0, 1, ŌłÆ1}.
proof_latex:
\begin{proof}
For any tree $t \neq \tau$, we have $\alpha^{(m)}(t) = r(t)^m \alpha(t) + Q_1$ and $\alpha^m(t) = m\alpha(t) + Q_2$, where $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are expressions involving $\alpha(u)$ for $r(u) < r(t)$. Suppose that $\alpha(u)$ has been proved equal to $E(u)$ for all such trees. Then
\begin{align*}
\alpha^{(m)}(t) &= r(t)^m \alpha(t) + Q_1, \\
\alpha^m(t) &= m\alpha(t) + Q_2, \\
E^{(m)}(t) &= r(t)^m E(t) + Q_1, \\
E^m(t) &= mE(t) + Q_2,
\end{align*}
so that $\alpha^{(m)}(t) = \alpha^m(t)$ implies that
\[
(r(t)^m - m)(\alpha(t) - E(t)) = 0,
\]
implying that $\alpha(t) = E(t)$, because $r(t)^m \neq m$ whenever $r(t) > 1$ and $m \notin \{0, 1, -1\}$.

Of the three excluded values of $m$ in Theorem~\ref{thm:387A}, only $m = -1$ is interesting. Methods for which $\alpha^{(-1)} = \alpha^{-1}$ have a special property which makes them of potential value as the source of efficient extrapolation procedures. Consider the solution of an initial value problem over an interval $[x_0, x]$ using $n$ steps of a Runge--Kutta method with stepsize $h = (x - x_0)/n$. Suppose the computed solution can be expanded in an asymptotic series in $h$,
\[
y(x) + \sum_{i=1}^\infty C_i h^i. \tag{387e}
\]
If the elementary weight function for the method is $\alpha$, then the method corresponding to $(\alpha^{(-1)})^{-1}$ exactly undoes the work of the method but with $h$ reversed. This means that the asymptotic error expansion for this reversed method would correspond to changing the sign of $h$ in \eqref{eq:387e}. If $\alpha = (\alpha^{(-1)})^{-1}$, this would give exactly the same expansion, so that \eqref{eq:387e} is an even function. It then becomes possible to extend the applicability of the method by extrapolation in even powers only.
\end{proof}

variables.name: E
variables.definition: E is an element of G1 (the group of mappings from trees to real numbers) representing the exact solution of a differential equation through one unit step h; E(t) gives the elementary weight for tree t.

variables.name: E^{(╬Ė)}
variables.definition: E^{(╬Ė)} represents the solution at a general point ╬Ėh advanced from the initial point; defined by E^{(╬Ė)}(t) = ╬Ė^{r(t)} E(t) for all t Ōłł T, where r(t) is the order of the tree t.

variables.name: T_k
variables.definition: T_k are elements of G1 (or mappings from trees to real numbers) appearing in the series expansion of E; specifically, E = 1 + sum_{k=1}^Ōł× (1/k!) T_k.

variables.name: G1
variables.definition: G1 is the group of mappings from the set of trees T to the real numbers, with a specific group operation (not fully detailed here but implied from context).

variables.name: Žä
variables.definition: Žä is the unique tree of order 1 (the single node tree).

variables.name: ╬▒^{(m)}
variables.definition: For ╬▒ in G1 and integer m, ╬▒^{(m)} is defined analogously to E^{(╬Ė)}: ╬▒^{(m)}(t) = m^{r(t)} ╬▒(t) for all t Ōłł T, where r(t) is the order of the tree t.

variables.name: ╬▒^m
variables.definition: For ╬▒ in G1 and integer m, ╬▒^m denotes the m-th power of ╬▒ under the group operation in G1.

equations.content: E = 1 + sum_{k=1}^Ōł× (1/k!) T_k, interpreted as E(t) = 1(t) + sum_{k=1}^Ōł× (1/k!) T_k(t) for any t Ōłł T.

preamble: The context involves the group G1 of mappings from trees to reals, with E representing the exact solution operator for ODEs. The notation ╬▒^{(m)} denotes scaling by m^{r(t)} on tree t, while ╬▒^m denotes group exponentiation. The theorem characterizes E by a property involving these operations for integers m not in {0,1,-1}.

dependencies.name: Exponential Function Class and Derivative Inclusion
